text:
University College Hospital 

National Hospital for Neurology and Neurosurgery 

Clinic Date: 02 October 2025 

Dr Susan  
University College Hospital  
National Hospital for Neurology and Neurosurgery, London WC1N 3BG  

Dear Dr Susan,  

William Harris, DOB: 15-02-1965, Hospital No: H563829 NHS No. 9273645810  
10 Meadow Park, London, EC1V 9BT 

Summary/ Diagnoses: Traumatic brain injury in 2014 with residual executive dysfunction and subjective memory decline/ recurrent seizures with recent clinical deterioration/ migraine with photophobia/ generalised anxiety symptoms/ moderate alcohol intake within recommended limits/non-smoker. 

Medication: Levetiracetam 1500 mg twice daily.  
Lamotrigine 100 mg twice daily.  
Propranolol 40 mg twice daily.  
Omeprazole 20 mg once daily.  
Cholecalciferol 800 IU once daily.  

I reviewed him in clinic today unaccompanied; he provided a detailed seizure log on his phone and permitted me to review entries from his workplace incident reports. He reports increasing daytime fatigue, morning headaches and intermittent tongue soreness after events. He continues to work full-time as a warehouse supervisor with early shifts and frequent overtime, and he describes a sustained period of high workload over the past three months. He feels events cluster on workdays, particularly on Mondays and Fridays following extended shifts and poor sleep. His mother, contacted by phone during the appointment with his consent, notes an overall improvement compared with previous years, with eight tonic seizures documented in 2019 so far; however, both he and I are concerned that the pattern over the last six weeks suggests deterioration. 

Seizures: Prior to the recent worsening, his diary entries were infrequent with long seizure-free intervals. Over the last six weeks there has been a clear escalation in frequency and recovery time. He now describes tonic-clonic weekly, often preceded by escalating irritability and a sensation of tightness in the jaw, followed by post-event confusion lasting 30–60 minutes and myalgia the next day. Events tend to occur in the late afternoon on workdays per patient report, with two episodes necessitating early departure from work and one requiring assistance from colleagues due to transient disorientation. There is no consistent aura reported. He denies missed doses but admits to variable sleep and irregular meal timing on shift days. No recent head injury or intercurrent illness. He does not drive. 

Examination: Alert and oriented. Speech fluent. Cranial nerves intact. Motor examination normal tone and power in all limbs, no focal deficits. Gait steady. No tongue laceration today. Cardiovascular and respiratory examinations unremarkable. 

Investigations: Last serum levels and safety bloods were in May 2025 (U&E, LFTs, FBC within reference). MRI brain (2018) showed post-traumatic changes in the right frontal lobe without progressive features. Ambulatory EEG (2022) did not capture a habitual event. He has not yet had recent video-EEG monitoring. 

Assessment: Clinical history indicates recent deterioration in seizure control with clustering on workdays and clear increase in event frequency, now described as tonic-clonic weekly. Likely multifactorial contribution from sleep deprivation, shift-related stress and irregular routines in the context of longstanding seizures after prior head injury. Safety risks at work have increased given postictal confusion and witnessed events on site. 

Plan: 
- Escalate lamotrigine to 150 mg twice daily over two weeks as per titration schedule provided today; continue levetiracetam 1500 mg twice daily. 
- Arrange urgent serum U&E, LFT, FBC, and trough levetiracetam/lamotrigine levels in 2–3 weeks after dose stabilisation. 
- Refer for video-EEG telemetry to characterise events and assess for optimisation of therapy. 
- Provide employer letter (with patient consent) outlining temporary adjustments: avoidance of lone working, working at ground level only, and scheduled rest breaks; discuss shift modification to reduce extended hours. 
- Reinforce seizure first aid and SUDEP risk information; patient provided with written safety advice including bathing, cooking, and workplace precautions. 
- Encourage consistent sleep routine, hydration, and regular meals; patient to continue seizure diary and record potential triggers. 
- Follow-up in 8 weeks, sooner if further escalation or injury occurs. 

Yours sincerely

check__Seizure Frequency Number.analysis: The letter explicitly states a current increase in seizure frequency: "now described as tonic-clonic weekly." This indicates approximately one tonic-clonic seizure per week in the recent period of deterioration (last six weeks). No other higher-frequency seizure type is mentioned. Therefore, the highest current frequency is 1 per week.
